Title: Structure and Biocatalytic Scope of Coclaurine N-Methyltransferase

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates ethyl- and allyl-AdoMet analogs in situ that enable CNMT-catalyzed N-alkyl diversification of norcoclaurine.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: coupled biocatalytic reaction with CNMT; product confirmation by HPLC and LCMS
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The mutant human methionine adenosyltransferase hMAT2A I322V was used to generate ethyl- and allyl-AdoMet analogs in situ from L-ethionine and S-allyl-L-homocysteine. 2. These cofactor analogs were accepted by CNMT to produce N-ethyl and N-allyl norcoclaurine. 3. The enzyme supports alkyl diversification rather than native plant nutrient metabolism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as an auxiliary biocatalytic gene in the study; not part of native BIA plant pathway core.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: midstream, key step toward the common branch-point intermediate (S)-reticuline
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: coclaurine; norcoclaurine; heliamine; other tetrahydroisoquinolines; AdoMet
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from S-adenosyl-L-methionine to the amine of coclaurine/norcoclaurine-like tetrahydroisoquinoline substrates.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms an essential methylated intermediate required for production of (S)-reticuline and many downstream BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step/essential enzyme in the core pathway to BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous overexpression in E. coli; X-ray crystallography; active-site mutagenesis; kinetic analysis; substrate screening; HPLC and LCMS product validation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes N-methylation of coclaurine-like substrates, enabling formation of methylated intermediates essential for bioactive BIA biosynthesis; also accepts multiple synthetic THIQ substrates and AdoMet analogs.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class I S-adenosyl-L-methionine-dependent methyltransferase; alpha/beta Rossmann fold
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: in vitro biocatalytic assays; cofactor analog supply with ethyl- and allyl-AdoMet
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Relatively low catalytic activity even with native substrates; promiscuity can generate side products in engineered microbial hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNMT from Coptis japonica is an essential enzyme in the core BIA pathway and introduces the N-methyl group needed for many bioactive alkaloids. 2. Crystal structures and mutagenesis identified E204 and H208 as critical catalytic residues, with aromatic residues such as W329 and F332 contributing to substrate binding. 3. CNMT shows relaxed specificity toward C1-substituted tetrahydroisoquinolines and accepts alternative AdoMet analogs, supporting biocatalytic diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for structure-guided engineering to improve activity and specificity for microbial BIA production and for synthesis of non-natural N-alkyl tetrahydroisoquinoline drugs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Both (S)- and (R)-coclaurine can be accommodated, indicating little stereoselectivity. The enzyme was studied primarily as a biocatalyst and pathway enzyme rather than for plant phenotype engineering.
